responseSet.title: ‘My Eyes are Misting Over’ Therapeutic Consultations in General Practice with a Child Presenting with an Eye Symptom
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘My Eyes are Misting Over’ Therapeutic Consultations in General Practice with a Child Presenting with an Eye Symptom

Sotiris Zalidis

Sotiris Zalidis

The Surgery, 52b Well Street, London E9 7PX.

Sotiris Zalidis  

This paper is written by a general practitioner with an interest in psychotherapy and psychosomatic medicine. It describes his response to the physical eye symptom ‘my eyes are misting over’ of a 10-year-old patient who was brought to the surgery by her mother who worried that her daughter might have an eye infection. This response was based on the recognition that emotions have physical components which sometimes are experienced in isolation and cannot be made sense of unless the emotional context in which they appear is understood. One of the commonest psychosomatic symptoms is tears, and unless the patient also feels the sorrow of which they are an integral part, he may not realise that he is crying, but may complain of misty vision, watery eyes, runny eyes, etc., and demand referral to an eye specialist.

In this case the initial understanding of the physical symptom as an expression of the crying response which was suppressed, was followed by three longer sessions during which the patient's emotions were explored. This was done by using Winnicott's squiggle method of communicating with children. This approach did not alter the family dynamics, or cure the patient, but helped her to understand and accept her symptom as an integral part of her emotional world, and guided her mother to accept that psychotherapy was probably the most appropriate way to deal with her daughter's problems.

responseSet.title: What Gets Measured, Gets Delivered?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Gets Measured, Gets Delivered?

Sue Robinson

Sue Robinson

Guildford, Surrey GU2 7LX.

Sue Robinson  

The government's work in reforming the NHS is being applied to psychiatry and four key principles have been identified: transparency, accessibility, accountability and protection. These principles are considered in relation to the problem of measurement and outcomes in one NHS psychotherapy context. Goodhart's ‘Law’ (what is counted, counts) is used to analyse how outcome measurements can impede the delivery of good services. The idea is proposed that what gets counted can become what is delivered in an area, such as psychoanalytic psychotherapy, where risk is essential to the process and the state of knowledge; and research is incomplete and uncertain.

Evidence-Based Practice: Issues for Psychotherapy

Martin Milton

Martin Milton

Upney Lane, Barking, IG11 9LX

Martin Milton  

This paper attempts to review both the frequently discussed ‘objective’ factors in evidence-based practice as well as the more subjective factors facing psychotherapists engaged in this debate. The contention is that greater familiarity with the issues will allow us to inform and structure this debate in a more appropriate manner than if we allow those from other disciplines to structure it for us.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Understanding of Violence and Suicide edited by R. J. Perelberg and L. Shengold (Routledge, London, 1998). 177 pp. £18.99.

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB

Paul Williams  

This is an excellent book. It is the outcome of a research project at the Anna Freud Centre into analyses of physically violent patients and it substantially furthers our psychoanalytic understanding of patients who are affected by violent and suicidal fantasies. The main credit for the book (which is published in the New Library of Psychoanalysis series) goes to the book's co-editor, Rosine Perelberg, who has chosen her contributors well and who provides an outstanding literature review on the subject. Perelberg has also written a clinical chapter (Chapter 4), which deals with the analysis of a violent patient who held the unconscious belief that he was born of violent intercourse between himself and his mother. This fantasy had attained the status of belief: that is, it was thought to be true while simultaneously known to be false. The chapter also discusses issues of technique in the treatment of such patients. It is essential to understand that the patient's experience in the analysis is that they are attempting to survive in the face of what they experience as a terrifying object.

All the chapters of the book are of a high quality—a rare achievement. Peter Fonagy and Mary Target discuss a male patient who was deformed from birth and suffered from attacks of spinal pain. The patient complained violently if Fonagy did not say or do anything to reduce the patient's pain during the sessions, and this seemed to reflect the way in which the patient as a child had berated his mother. Fonagy's tolerance and analytic consistency enabled this persecuted and persecuting state of affairs to improve. This contrasted starkly with a representation of the mother in the transference as a resentful and rather ignorant person who viewed her child's problems with distaste, thereby exacerbating the patient's physical and mental distress.

Donald Campbell addresses the role of the father in a pre-suicidal state, and the use of attacks on the body to get rid of painful mental states. Campbell describes how the body can be treated like an object and concretely identified with the lost and hated person. In the wish to commit suicide there can be discerned an additional wish for the surviving self to merge with an idealized maternal figure.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imperviousness in Anorexia—The No-Entry Defence

Alexandra Willner

Alexandra Willner

St. Ann's Road, London N15 3TH

Alexandra Willner  

The concept of imperviousness in the ontogeny and progression of anorexia nervosa is explored in relation to a patient in her 30s, with longstanding and severe anorexia. Katie's history is traced from the early years, through adolescence into adulthood and the ensuing therapy. The relevance of lack of containment in setting up a ‘no-entry’ defence is considered in relation to the pervasive imperviousness displayed in anorexia. Some ideas derived from psychoanalytic and other clinical literature are discussed in relation to anorexia nervosa.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Menopause: Dignity and Development at the end of the Reproductive Cycle

Anne Zachary MB FRCPsych

Anne Zachary

8 Fitzjohns Avenue, London NW3 5NA.

Anne Zachary  

There is a historical literature on the menopause, although this has been bound within the taboo that the subject has always represented. The availability of Hormone Replacement Therapy has played a part in challenging the taboo of the menopause so that it can now be more openly addressed. This paper presents clinical material collected over considerable time that mirrors both the emergence of the subject into greater focus and the development of the author as a clinician. Originally a research project during training, it offers an economic model of working through a potentially painful life event, to discover creative opportunities. This involves acknowledging some of the emotional difficulties that include dealing with issues such as loss, envy and death. Whether the patient brings this passage to treatment as a problem, or whether it occurs during the course of treatment for other, overt reasons, it offers women a chance to negotiate a greater psychological maturity by making use of an in-built biological advantage.

Teaching Psychiatric Patients: A Psychoanalytic Study

Olivia Sagan

Olivia Sagan

46 Elmdale Road, London N13 4UL.

Olivia Sagan  

This paper attempts to describe some of the challenges of teaching psychiatric patients within a hospital environment. It uses post-Kleinian ideas to look at the unconscious processes at work within the learning/teaching dynamic and observes the psychotic mind's unique approach to learning. The central hypothesis of the paper is that psychoanalytic reflection of educational processes within a psychiatric institution is of clear benefit to both teacher and learner, and can also forestall several of the more problematic or even harmful outcomes of the increasing trend to ‘educate’ the patient.

Geoffrey Pearson  

Edward Glover, according to this biography, is one of the neglected pioneers and champions of psychoanalysis in Britain. Born in 1888, he underwent medical training at the University of Glasgow, and entered analysis in the early 1920s with Karl Abraham at the newly-formed Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute. Glover was Secretary of the International Psychoanalytic Association from 1934 to 1944, the year in which he resigned from the British Association following the acrimonious disputes that shook British psychoanalysis over the work of Melanie Klein. He nevertheless remained active within the IPA, owing to his eligibility as a full member of the Swiss Society. He wrote various papers and books, some on analytic technique that are still apparently well regarded in the USA, although less so in Britain, where his contribution has been largely forgotten.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Struggle for Communication with a Patient in an Intensive NHS Psychotherapy

‘Speech in their Dumbness, Language in their Very Gesture’—The Winter's Tale Act V, Scene 2

Rose Golberg

Rose Golberg

1 Summerfield Avenue, London NW 6 6JT.

Rose Golberg  

In this paper, Bion's triad of papers ‘On arrogance’, ‘Attacks on linking’ and ‘A theory of thinking’ (1957, 1959, 1962) are drawn on to trace through and discuss how patient and psychotherapist learned to work together in the course of a three-times-a-week NHS psychotherapy, which lasted for just under two-and-a-half years, terminating with the ending of the therapist's training. Attention is paid to the constant oscillations of work with the patient, a young man, in which his narcissistic and grandiose defences, while crude and precarious, were clung to by him, and returned to throughout the work but with lessening conviction. These often apparently random oscillations could be understood in terms of what Bion describes as apparently scattered references to curiosity, arrogance and stupidity, which none the less provide the evidence of a continuing psychological disaster. Throughout were reminders of the limits to an NHS training psychotherapy, which could not be open-ended and which was brought to an end at a point at which the patient and psychotherapist had learned to talk to each other.

responseSet.title: Michael B. Conran 1924-2001
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michael B. Conran 1924-2001

Noel Hess and Emanuel Lewis

Emanuel Lewis

Emanuel Lewis  

Michael Conran, who died on 19 December 2001 at the age of 77 years, was a founding member of the APP; he served on the first Council and continued to be active in the organization until a few months before his death. He was a Trustee of the APP Educational Trust until he resigned his position, because of his ill-health, in October. He told me, at the time, that he found this a very difficult decision to make, because of his parental affection for the organization; to sever an active relationship of some 20 years was sad and painful. He also contributed to APP conferences and to this journal, although his published work is a small proportion of his papers, which he preferred to deliver before a live audience.

Michael's early life was spent in Brighton, and he retained a great fondness for it, acquiring a good deal of its raffish charm for himself. He was the elder of two children, with a younger sister to whom he was very close, until her death about 4 years ago. He studied medicine at Guy's and, after a short spell in the RAF, went into academic work, lecturing and researching in microbiology at the University of Birmingham. In his 40s, he decided to change career; having had a long-standing intellectual interest in psychoanalysis he moved to London in order to have analysis himself and to apply to train at the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. He weathered initial rejections and persevered in this ambition, which was extremely important to him, so that he was eventually accepted to train, and he qualified as an analyst in the early 1970s.

During this period of his life he was working at Shenley Hospital, where his Consultant was Tom Hayward, someone who became his teacher, mentor and much-loved friend. He inherited a ward of young male schizophrenics from David Cooper. This ward—Villa 21—was where he developed his abiding interest in the dynamics of psychosis and the ætiology of psychotic breakdown within the family. This was pioneering work, as the application of psychoanalytic understanding to inpatient work with psychotic patients and their families was possibly even rarer then than it is now.

[[RunningHead]]

Happiness, Death, and the Remainder of Life by Jonathan Lear (Harvard University Press, Cambridge & London, 2000). 189 pp. £16.96.

Robin Cooper

Robin Cooper

14 Dartmouth Road, London NW2 4EU

Robin Cooper  

‘To live with human possibility’, writes Jonathan Lear in the concluding sentences of this book, ‘one has to tolerate a peculiar kind of theoretical anxiety: the willingness to live without a principle’. The route that Lear follows in elucidation of this far-reaching and important claim takes us through one of western philosophy's foundational texts, Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics, to Freud's Beyond the Pleasure Principle. Specifically, Lear shows us some difficulties Aristotle and Freud encounter in their respective reluctances to live without a principle, how each of these sustained attempts to provide a comprehensive teleology of human life collapses, and, more importantly, how instructively their attempts to subsume human striving under a principle fail.

Let us start with Aristotle, where Lear gets to business with the very first—and famously problematic—sentence of the Nicomachean Ethics. By introducing ‘the good’ at this point, Aristotle is participating in what Lear calls ‘a peculiar kind of inaugural instantiation’; he is ‘trying to introduce the concept by means of which it would make sense for humans to take a teleologically oriented interest in their lives’. What Aristotle is doing is important here, what he is drawing us into. This emphasis upon the performative aspect of Aristotle's writing is made clearer with the introduction of happiness, for here, as Lear elegantly demonstrates, Aristotle shows himself to be performing a ‘seduction’. Aristotle is seducing us with enigmatic signifiers, which, like Lacan's hieroglyphs in the desert, we recognize as having a meaning—indeed a special meaning for us—but whose content we do not understand. Reducing 60 closely-reasoned pages to a few sentences, Lear's argument roughly is that Aristotle injects ‘happiness’ as a concept in terms of which one's entire life can and should be evaluated. This telos ‘happiness’, by which we are asked to evaluate human life, lies forever beyond the horizon, beyond the teleological principle. It is not simply that ‘happiness’ somehow eludes us as we chase it, but that as we take it out of its ordinary unreflective home (where we all know that if we could only have … we would be happy)

responseSet.title: Supervision of Residents Training for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in a General Hospital: The Impact on the Patient
responseSet.authorMast: Charalambos S. Ierodiakonou and  Ioanna Ierodiakonou-Benou
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Supervision of Residents Training for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in a General Hospital: The Impact on the Patient

Charalambos Ierodiakonou and Ioanna Ierodiakonou-Benou

Ioanna Ierodiakonou-Benou

4 Aristotle Square, Thessaloniki, Greece

Ioanna Ierodiakonou-Benou  

The special circumstances and problems of supervision of residents in Psychiatry undergoing training in individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy for adult patients in a General Hospital are presented under three headings: (1) the setting, i.e. the clinical structure in which supervision takes place and the patients are available in a General Hospital, (2) the trainees and their training programme, and (3) the interactions between supervisor and trainee. Among the issues taken up for discussion are: omnipotent fantasies of the trainee and/or his patient regarding the Hospital's ‘potential for a cure’; problems during a teaching supervision in the supervisor-trainee relationship when the latter is not in personal therapy; projection of responsibilities to the supervisor as if the supervisor personified the Hospital, or oral needs of dependence; identification of the trainee with a former inpatient and supportive tendencies; difficulties in dropping ‘quick’ hospital drug-treatment methods; the supervisor's narcissistic problems; relatives’ interventions with the ‘hospital doctor’.

Confidentiality and Mental Health edited by Christopher Cordess (Jessica Kingsley, London, 2001) 207 pp. £15.95.

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy

Ham Common, Richmond, Surrey, TW 10 7JF

Kevin Healy  

Why all the current fuss about confidentiality and mental health? Confidentiality is a burning issue on the Council of the Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy. Council members are bothered by the implications of the Data Protection Act, which makes therapists’ process notes of sessions part of the official medical notes. They are further bothered by impending legislative changes, broader issues concerning confidentiality of clinical notes, and confidentiality concerning the publication of patient material in the APP Journal and elsewhere. By the time you read this review, there will have been a lecture, a response and a debate at the APP Annual General Meeting concerning confidentiality. While it certainly is a burning issue, is it also a hot potato? Hot potatoes need to cool before they can be eaten as a source of energy and nourishment.

Feelings become inflamed around the issue of confidentiality. Ruth Wyner, who reviewed this book for Therapeutic Communities, was imprisoned for keeping to her understanding of confidentiality. (Details of the history of this case and some of the disturbing issues it raised are available on the ‘Cambridge Two’ website, http://www.cambridgetwo.com). Christopher Cordess, who delivered the APP annual lecture on confidentiality, is also editor of this stimulating book, Confidentiality and Mental Health. It struck me on reading his contribution that he is clearly bothered, concerned and worried about the issue of confidentiality. Some chapter authors highlight more the confusion and angst around the issue, while others present clear pragmatic guidance on possible ways forward. Those who work currently in multidisciplinary teams seemed less worried about theoretical and philosophical issues surrounding confidentiality. Other authors were clearly less confident about the merits of multi-agency working. This was reflected in their views on confidentiality. Social workers are more adept at multi-agency working, at not acting in isolation, and perhaps in recognizing that the law rarely provides direct answers. Those working in the legal field contributed clearly thought-out and clearly written chapters that seemed less fuelled by emotional issues.

André Green at the Squiggle Foundation edited by Jan Abram (Karnac Books, London, 2000). 116 pp. £13.95.

Kamaldeep Bhui

Kamaldeep Bhui

London E1 4NS

Kamaldeep Bhui  

This engaging volume edited by Jan Abram is a collection of lectures given by André Green with a particular emphasis on Winnicott's writings and work. The Squiggle Foundation is dedicated to thinking and discussion of Winnicott's work, and in this volume André Green conveys his interpretation of Winnicott's work and how his own thinking has built on the writings of Winnicott.

There are five lectures, as well as the editor's informative foreword. The lecture titles are:

Experience and Thinking in Analytic Practice;

Object(s) and Subject;

On Thirdness;

The Posthumous Winnicott: on Human Nature;

The Intuition of the Negative in Playing and Reality.

Jan Abrams's foreword captures both the joy and the struggle of reading this volume. In particular, the opening paragraphs outline how Alexander Newman, the first founder and director of Squiggle, on introducing André Green to an audience announced how he had

read André Green's books from cover to cover with little understanding and much interest.

Although these comments were made in a light-hearted exchange, there is a great deal in the book that is engaging and of immense interest; but occasionally the dense theoretical hypothesis-building makes it difficult fully to understand on first reading — perhaps even on a second or third reading.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

The potency of our new partnership with our publishers, Taylor & Francis, is beginning to become apparent. Thanks to many of you for your favourable comments on the journal's new presentation. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy is now available on line to subscribers and the T&F marketing machine enables us to be represented throughout the publishing world, as well as at major conferences at home and overseas. We have recently reached a new agreement with the Tavistock Clinic, whereby the journal will be actively promoted at all Tavistock events. If all goes well, these combined measures should enable Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy to reach a larger readership, which will in turn promote an increased interest in the APP itself.

We continue to receive a good flow of high-quality papers, and the generous Essay Prize proposed by the APP Educational Trust will no doubt help this to continue. This issue, the second of volume 16 of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy seems to me to exemplify what the APP and its journal, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, is all about. We have a paper by Rose Golberg which is a detailed discussion of intensive, long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy with a very ill NHS patient. Alexandra Wilner describes and analyses the treatment and management of a patient within a specialist NHS psychiatric setting. Bob Hinshelwood and Wilhelm Skogstad write on the use of psychoanalytic observational studies in the training of psychiatrists. Martin Milton offers a detailed review and evaluation of the current debates on ‘evidence-based practice’ and their applicability to psychoanalytic psychotherapy. As always, we have an excellent range of first-class book reviews. It is always a bit of a struggle to maintain the balance between clinical psychotherapy, psychotherapeutic management, innovative training initiatives and current research preoccupations within the NHS and the public sector in general. In this issue I believe we have succeeded.

We cannot, however, afford to rest on our laurels. Next year we move to four issues per annum. We need to improve still more the input of papers if our standards are not to drop. Please write for us and encourage your colleagues to write for us. It can feel intimidating to submit a paper for the first time, particularly as most of you are clinicians rather than academics. We do understand this, and will do all we can to help authors get papers to a publishable standard. Please feel free to talk to me or any member of the Editorial Board informally, before making a formal submission. Although we are determined to raise the profile of the journal and of the work which is taking place in the public sector, there is no sense in which we wish to move away from our predominantly APP readership. On the contrary: the journal exists to promote your work and it relies on you write about it for us.

[[RunningHead]]

Analysis and its Modifications — Work in a School

Katherine Arnold

Katherine Arnold

57 Cecile Park, London N8 9AX

Katherine Arnold  

Work as a school psychotherapi st in a secondary school is described, with the aim of considering the varied ways in which the provision was used by staff and students. A brief view is given of the history of work in school in the development of psychoanalysis. The creation of an analytic space in school is described. The contribution made to the individual's psychological growth, of a space in which to think, freely emerges; and the ways in which the simplicity of the analytic setting allows its adaptation, by the institution and by the individual, to meet the particular developmental needs of the moment, are shown.

Pearl King

Pearl King

112a Shirland Road, London

Pearl King  

The author of this book has set out to describe to his readers the coming into being and the dying away of two remarkable ‘experiments’ that took place in a military hospital in England during World War II, and which, soon after the War, became a very powerful ‘shared group experience’ that empowered those professionals who took part in it; while those who did not do so, endowed those who were engaged in ‘whatever happened there’ with what seemed like ‘a special professional qualification’.

As I have known the main characters in this book, but never worked in Northfield Hospital, I must include myself among those who endowed those who did work there, with ‘a special professional qualification’. I met most of the colleagues described in this book during the period when they had left, or were leaving, Northfield Hospital in 1945-46. They were keen to talk about their experiences and to give me their point of view about what had taken place; and I, who had been working in a munitions factory, was hungry to hear what they had to tell me.

I therefore found this a very interesting book to read, and it made me realise how much can be missed if you only rely on ‘the oral tradition’, which John Rickman himself valued so highly. If you do, you may not get a clear picture of the structures within which events take place and which helps to relate individuals to each other, and which also contains the intra-group tensions that give buoyancy to what happens within the structure.

From the history of what happened as recorded in this book, and particularly from its carefully recorded data, I was able to fill in various facts, to understand more clearly the sequence of events and relate them to what was going on in the War at the time, which helped to make sense of what I already knew.

R. Hinshelwood and Wilhelm Skogstad

Wilhelm Skogstad

18 Artesian Road, London W2 5AR

1 Ham Common, Richmond, Surrey TW 10 7JF

Wilhelm Skogstad  

The authors describe the history and underlying ideas of a project to observe health-care organizations. Staff in medical and psychiatric institutions are faced with major anxieties in their work with ill people and these may be dealt with in various more or less defensive ways. It is argued that institutional observation can be a tool to detect and understand the dynamics, defensive techniques and culture of a health-care organization, and that for those working in such institutions it can be a useful learning instrument. Two observations, of a mental health hostel and a medical ward, are described in some detail.

Learning Disability as a Refuge from Knowledge

David Simpson

David Simpson

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA
e-mail: learndis@tavi-port.nhs.uk

David Simpson  

In this paper the author finds that an important factor preventing learning and development in learning disabled people is the way in which their learning disability can act as a refuge from knowledge. He begins by discussing how the very term ‘Learning Disability’, through obscuring the distinction between organic and psychological difficulties in learning, can itself become a refuge for those who use the term from facing the reality of damage and deficiency. Following this, he considers the development of psychological impairments to learning from a psychoanalytical perspective. He places particular emphasis on how the emotional consequences of a child's handicap, to the child him/herself and his/her parents, can affect the development of curiosity and learning. The author illustrates these ideas with clinical material from individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy with an adolescent boy with a learning disability.

Releasing the Self. The Healing Legacy of Heinz Kohut by Phil Mollon (Whurr Publishers, London, 2001). xii + 240 pp. £18.50.

Rose Stockwell

Rose Stockwell

PO Box 196, Shrewsbury, SY1 2XF

Rose Stockwell  

‘In Britain there is a curious tendency to pronounce Kohut's name incorrectly as “Cahoot”. The correct pronunciation, as used by American colleagues, is simply Ko-(as in low)-hut’ (p. xi). This is just one of the distortions about Kohut and his work that Phil Mollon endeavours to clarify for us. As he says in the Preface:

It has seemed to me that Kohut's theories & insights have in many ways been eclipsed before they have been fully understood & integrated within psychoanalysis … my reason for writing the present book was to revisit in depth & detail, Kohut's own accounts of his theory & clinical work, and, in so doing, to interact with his ideas, linking them with contemporary perspectives within psychoanalysis

(p. xi).

I wanted to read this book because I had been exposed to ideas that purported to be extensions of Kohut's work that I had felt uncomfortable with and I wanted to know more about Kohut's original work. I was also attracted to the book because Mollon is a British analyst and I hoped that he would contextualize Kohut's American work within a British framework. It was an extra bonus that Mollon linked Kohut's work to a multiplicity of contemporary psychoanalytical ideas, some of them American and European, as well as recent British developments. The book is a tour de force, introducing the reader to much of Kohut's written work and to an extensive vocabulary of terms such as ‘selfobject’, ‘transmuting internalization’, ‘inherent developmental agenda’, ‘compensatory development’, and many more. Concepts and terms are made accessible through clinical and theoretical discussion, and in some cases arguments are developed with different schools of psychoanalytic thought, which enable the reader to place the work in relation to other developments both historically and conceptually. Some readers will be aware that Mollon has already written extensively about Kohut and the application of his work and is well qualified to write the current book. The disadvantage of his thorough knowledge is that, just occasionally, he assumes knowledge and familiarity with the original texts that his readers may not have.

Christina Wieland   Psychoanalytic psychotherapist in private practice, author of The Undead Mother: Psychoanalytic explorations of masculinity, femininity and matricide. (Rebus Press 2000, reprinted by Karnac 2002)

This is a book on fatherhood and on the father-son relationship through the ages. It is a history of this relationship and of the genealogy of men, and discusses the father as an institution that promotes a mature male identity: the father as an internal object for the son, the father as a function, the father as the parent of civilization.

Zoja is a Jungian analyst and the book has a distinct Jungian flavour to it, although there is no particular Jungian terminology in it. It is well written and makes at times fascinating reading, especially in its analysis of classical texts such as Homer, Virgil and, more recently, John Steinbeck's The Grapes of Wrath.

It is not a psychoanalytic book; in fact at times it is surprisingly non-psychoanalytic, and the discussion of Freud and Klein is very sketchy and rather simplistic. Yet the book is interesting despite its several problems: its lack of any analysis of the internal world; its idealization of the father-son relationship within patriarchy; its lack of any discussion of the relationship between mother and father; its lack of any discussion of a parental couple or any in-depth discussion of the Oedipus complex (this latter is especially surprising in a book about the father, even if the author is Jungian). There is generally a nostalgia for old patriarchal relationships. However, the main argument of the book holds, i.e. that male identity vacillates between, on the one hand, a narcissistic, irresponsible, impulsive, adolescent type of personality in which hubris predominates and the responsible, provident father on the other. When the authority of the father declines, the society of brothers dominated by the immature male personality prevails and ruthless competition and the survival of the fittest threaten to plunge humankind into the law of the jungle.

The book is divided into four parts. The first part entitled ‘Prehistory’ ventures into evolutionary psychology and into the animal roots of human experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Remaking Connections: Refugees and the Development of ‘Emotional Capital’ in Therapy Groups11

Caroline Garland , Francesca Hume and Sarah Majid

Sarah Majid

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA

Sarah Majid  

Many asylum-seekers arrive in this country in a traumatized state. Much psychic work, including mourning for the old lost life, has to take place before the individual can make use of the social and economic opportunities, such as they are, eventually afforded by the host culture. We describe a form of ongoing specialized psychoanalytic group therapy that addresses the psychological issues that can impede the individual's integration into a new country. The concept of ‘emotional capital’ is introduced as a way of summarizing those internal capacities we see as fundamental to the individual's capacity to make use of what Putnam (2000) has termed ‘social capital’. Asylum-seekers from several countries with only extremely limited English in common are seen together in an ongoing therapy group, where they may remain for over a year, before they are ready (emotionally and linguistically) to join a heterogeneous psychoanalytic psychotherapy group within the same Department. Clinical material is presented from the two settings: the Refugees’ Group, to illustrate the first steps in the rebuilding of a capacity to make emotional connections and the later Analytic Therapy Group where refugees’ earlier developmental difficulties can be addressed within the new social context.

The other Patient

Christo Joannidis

Christo Joannidis

79 Belsize Lane London NW 3 5AU
e-mail: ch.joannidis@virgin.net

Christo Joannidis  

This paper argues for the widening of perspective in the understanding of the functioning of the analytic process. By focusing on the complementary half of the bi-directional communication from one unconscious to another — the half that remains outside the analyst's conscious understanding or experience — it advocates that the conceptualization of the work we do can be enriched without recourse to changes in technique. Refusing to lose sight of the fact that the analyst's unconscious is, unbeknownst to him, constantly conveying messages to the analysand who then responds in accordance with these communications. This can only deepen our appreciation of the analysand's internal world and of the process of analysis. A selection of writings by psychoanalytic, philosophical and infant psychology authors as well as clinical material is enlisted to support the argument.

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA

Marilyn Lawrence  

It was with a pleasurable sense of anticipation that I approached this much-heralded translation of what is regarded as a classic work on psychosis in the French tradition, commissioned by the New International Library of Psychoanalysis.

Alan Sheridan is the translator of much of the work of Lacan as well as that of Foucault, ‘le maître véritable’. The book opens with a characteristically lucid account of Aulagnier's ideas by Joyce McDougall and Nathalie Zaltman. This is very helpful, and Susan Budd, the editor responsible for this book, is to be commended for it.

The author's aim she states as finding ‘a way of analysing the psychotic's relationship to discourse that would bring psychoanalytic practice closer to achieving the ambition of its project’ (p. xxv). She says that she had intended Part One to be merely an introduction, setting out the concepts to be used in her elaboration of psychotic discourse in Part Two.

Sexuality: Psychoanalytic Perspectives edited by Celia Harding (Hove and Philadelphia: Brunner-Routledge, 2001). xi + 201 pp. £15.99.

Deborah Goodes

Deborah Goodes

Whitechapel Road, London E1

Deborah Goodes  

‘Sexuality’—the word is writ large over a cover illustration of overlapping faces, bodies and, possibly, body parts: the kind of part-figurative collage that, for the last 30 years or so, has been used to signify that ‘modern’ ideas are contained within a certain kind of textbook. On one level this juxtaposition of the tantalizing word with the safe design gives the reader a sense of what to expect; it is a book about sex, but it is not provocative. Debate hots up as we reach the middle chapters, but the tone is always thoughtful. Clinical material is far from sensational; vignettes illustrating theory in practice rather than offering to share the intensity of the psychoanalytic work. The book exemplifies what psychoanalysis is good at; it is thorough—within its parameters—and it invites us to make our own connections, to draw together the issues raised by the 10 contributors. It encourages a reasonable, flexible and open-minded approach to the material and its application in our work.

As a textbook, this publication is very welcome and, in fact, as Celia Harding tells us, the contributions started life as part of a lecture series. It provides a lively and straightforward overview of psychoanalytic theories of sexuality (particularly in Robert Young's first chapter)
